coredata.dc:title: Colorimetric and fluorometric dual-mode sensing of cefadroxil based on peroxidase-like activity of iron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Alsharif, Shaker T.
Ali, Al-Montaser Bellah H.
El-Wekil, Mohamed M.
coredata.dc:description: Herein, new iron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (NCDs-Fe) were prepared, exhibiting strong peroxidase-mimetic activity and a photoluminescence quantum yield of 28.76 %. In the presence of NCDs-Fe, an oxidative coupling reaction between 4-aminoantipyrine (4-ANP) and cefadroxil (CFD), a phenolic cephalosporin, was facilitated by H2O2, resulting in red-colored product with absorbance at 510 nm. The absorbance of this red-colored product increased proportionally with rising CFD concentrations, serving as the basis for the colorimetric measurement. Additionally, the resultant red color ceased the fluorescence of NCDs-Fe at 460 nm, forming the basis of the fluorometric measurement. The variables affecting the synthesis and detection of CFD were carefully optimized. Under optimized conditions, the absorbance values increased with CFD concentrations in the range of 0.05–400 µM, with LOD value of 0.02 µM. For the fluorometric method, the response ratio increased with rising CFD’s concentrations from 0.01 to 400 µM with LOD of 0.004 µM. The NCDs-Fe was applied to detect CFD in real samples, achieving recovery rates of 96.5 % to 104.0 % with RSD values below 4.12 %.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cefadroxil
Peroxidase-mimetic
Oxidative coupling
Dual-mode
NCDs-Fe

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Peroxidase-like activity of NCDs and NCDs-Fe

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM (inset is HRTEM), (B) particle distribution, (C) XRD (C), and EDX (D) of NCDs-Fe.
(A) FT-IR and (B) Raman spectra of NCDs and NCDs-Fe.
(A) UV/Vis and fluorescence spectra of NCDs-Fe under standard excitation conditions. (B) Excitation-dependent emission spectra of NCDs-Fe.
Responses of different peroxidase-mimetic platforms for detecting 50 µM CFD.

(A) Absorption spectra of NCDs-Fe/4-ANP/H2O2 system for detection of 0.05–400 µM CFD; (B) Plot relationship absorbance vs. CFD concentration; (C) Fluorescence spectra of NCDs-Fe/4-ANP/H2O2 system for detection of 0.02–400 µM CFD; (D) Plot (F0/F) vs. CFD concentration.

Selectivity and interference analysis of NCDs-Fe for detecting 20 µM CFD (1) in the presence of a 25-fold excess of interfering species, including ions (2–12) (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Hg2+, Co2+, Cl-, SO4 2−, CO3 2−), small biomolecules (13–17) (dopamine, ascorbic acid, uric acid, glutathione, and cysteine), and antibiotics (18–32) (penicillin, ampicillin, cefazolin, cephalexin, cefuroxime, cefaclor, cefoxitin, ceftriaxone, cefotaxime, ceftazidime, cefdinir, cefepime, cefixime, ceftobiprole, and ceftaroline fosamil).
Comparison between proposed dual-mode detection and other methods for CFD detection.
Detection of CFD in pharmaceutical tablets (n = 3).
Detection of CFD in serum samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Colorimetric and fluorometric dual-mode sensing of cefadroxil based on peroxidase-like activity of iron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Science Department, College of Pharmacy, Umm AlQura University, Makkah 21955, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pharmaceutical Science Department, College of Pharmacy, Umm AlQura University, Makkah 21955, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, new iron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (NCDs-Fe) were prepared, exhibiting strong peroxidase-mimetic activity and a photoluminescence quantum yield of 28.76 %. In the presence of NCDs-Fe, an oxidative coupling reaction between 4-aminoantipyrine (4-ANP) and cefadroxil (CFD), a phenolic cephalosporin, was facilitated by H2O2, resulting in red-colored product with absorbance at 510 nm. The absorbance of this red-colored product increased proportionally with rising CFD concentrations, serving as the basis for the colorimetric measurement. Additionally, the resultant red color ceased the fluorescence of NCDs-Fe at 460 nm, forming the basis of the fluorometric measurement. The variables affecting the synthesis and detection of CFD were carefully optimized. Under optimized conditions, the absorbance values increased with CFD concentrations in the range of 0.05–400 µM, with LOD value of 0.02 µM. For the fluorometric method, the response ratio increased with rising CFD’s concentrations from 0.01 to 400 µM with LOD of 0.004 µM. The NCDs-Fe was applied to detect CFD in real samples, achieving recovery rates of 96.5 % to 104.0 % with RSD values below 4.12 %.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The widespread presence of antibiotics in the environment has raised serious concerns due to their role in promoting antimicrobial resistance, disrupting ecosystems, and posing health hazards [1,2]. Among these, cephalosporins are frequently detected because of their extensive clinical use. As β-lactam antibiotics, they inhibit bacterial cell wall synthesis by targeting penicillin-binding proteins [5]. However, inappropriate usage and disposal contribute significantly to their environmental accumulation [3,4], leading to both ecological and therapeutic challenges, including drug resistance and hypersensitivity reactions [6–8].

Cefadroxil (CFD) is a first-generation cephalosporin used to treat various infections, including those of the skin, throat, and urinary tract [9]. Its detection in pharmaceutical and biological matrices is important for both quality control and pharmacokinetic monitoring. Conventional CFD detection methods include chromatography, spectrophotometry, and electrochemical sensing [10–14]. Although chromatographic techniques offer high accuracy and selectivity, they require costly equipment, long analysis times, and extensive sample preparation [15,16]. Spectrophotometric methods are simpler and faster but often suffer from limited selectivity and sensitivity when used alone [17,18]. Electrochemical approaches provide good sensitivity, yet face challenges with sensor fouling and selectivity, especially in complex matrices [19,20].

To address these limitations, hybrid or dual-mode sensing platforms are increasingly explored to enhance detection performance. Among these, carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as promising materials due to their low cost, water solubility, photostability, and biocompatibility [21–26]. Their fluorescence tunability and potential to mimic enzyme-like activities make them attractive for sensing applications [27–30]. Doping CDs with metal ions or heteroatoms such as nitrogen and iron can significantly enhance their catalytic properties and introduce new functionalities [31,32]. Previous studies have utilized Fe-doped N-doped carbon dots (NCDs-Fe) for various analytes based on peroxidase-mimetic activity. For instance, Alqahtani et al. [21] developed NCDs-Fe for the colorimetric and fluorometric detection of H2O2 and glucose using 4-ANP and phenol as chromogenic substrates. Wang et al. synthesized NCDs-Fe for the dual-mode detection of dopamine, employing a tetramethylbenzidine /H2O2 reaction system [33]. Another work by Wang et al. reported the use of NCDs-Fe for detecting H2O2 and xanthine, utilizing p-phenylenediamine as the color-developing agent [34].

4-Aminoantipyrine (4-ANP) is widely used in analytical chemistry for oxidative coupling reactions, particularly in the colorimetric and fluorometric detection of phenolic compounds and drugs. It serves as a key reagent that forms colored quinoneimine derivatives upon reaction with phenolic groups in the presence of oxidants like hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and catalysts (e.g., nanozymes or peroxidases) [35,36]. It reacts with phenolic compounds to produce intensely colored products (quinoneimines), which are easily measurable spectrophotometrically. Therefore, it was used for determination of phenol-containing compounds such as phenol [36], amoxicillin [37], and epinephrine [38].

In this study, we synthesized iron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (NCDs-Fe) with strong peroxidase-like activity and high photoluminescence. We propose a dual-mode colorimetric and fluorometric platform for the sensitive detection of CFD. The platform leverages both the catalytic and fluorescent properties of NCDs-Fe to improve selectivity and detection range. Despite spectrophotometry’s known limitations, it was retained in this study as a complementary mode, providing visual confirmation and supporting the fluorometric data, which offers higher sensitivity and lower detection limits. No prior fluorometric or colorimetric method for cefadroxil (CFD) detection based on peroxidase-mimetic activity has been reported in the literature. This study introduces a novel NCDs-Fe nanozyme that outperforms previously reported Fe-doped carbon dots in several aspects: (1) exhibits stronger peroxidase-like catalytic activity, (2) maintains high stability over extended periods and under various storage conditions, (3) retains strong and stable photoluminescence signals, enhancing sensitivity in optical assays, and (4) enables dual-mode (colorimetric and fluorometric) detection, improving reliability of the determination.

Cefadroxil monohydrate (CFD, 98.8 %), dopamine (DA, 97.7 %), ascorbic acid (AA, 98.5 %), uric acid (UA, 98.7 %), glucose (97.6 %), glutathione (GSH, 97.6 %), cysteine (Cys, 97.4 %), pencillin (98.4 %), ampicillin (98.5 %), cefazolin (98.4 %), cephalexin (97.4 %), cefuroxime (98.7 %), cefaclor (97.6 %), cefoxitin (97.7 %), ceftriaxone (99.3 %), cefotaxime (98.5 %), ceftazidime (98.7 %), Cefdinir (98.6 %), cefepime (98.5 %), cefixime (99.3 %), ceftobiprole (97.6 %), ceftaroline fosamil (98.4 %), 4-aminoantipyrine (4-ANP), tetramethylbenzidine (TMB, 98.7 %), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), ferric chloride (FeCl3·6H2O), copper chloride tetrahydrate (CuCl2·4H2O), manganese chloride tetrachloride (MnCl2·4H2O), cobalt chloride tetrahydrate (CoCl2·4H2O), nickel chloride tetrahydrate (NiCl2·H2O), ethylenediamine (AR), K2CO3, KHCO3, CH3COOH, and CH3COONa, were procured from Sigma Aldrich.
Supplementary materials contain instrument information.

Cefadroxil (CFD) stock solutions (10-4 M) were prepared by dissolving the appropriate amount in water and diluted to desired concentrations. 4-Aminoantipyrine (4-ANP, 10.0 mM) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 0.4 mM) were freshly prepared in double distilled water prior to use. Buffer solutions were prepared using standard protocols: carbonate buffer (pH 10.0) from K2CO3 and KHCO3, and acetate buffer (pH 3.6) from acetic acid and sodium acetate. All interfering substances were dissolved at 1.0 mM for selectivity studies.

The NCDs-Fe was synthesized via a simple hydrothermal method. The optimized procedure involved dissolving 1.2 g of FeCl3•6H2O in 25 mL of deionized water (DI) using ultrasonication. Subsequently, 0.50 g of glucose and 0.30 mL of ethylenediamine were added to the solution. Then, it was subjected to autoclaving at 220 °C for 12 h. The cooled mixture was centrifuged (5000 rpm), then dialyzed for 24 h using a 2000 Da MWCO membrane, replacing the external water every 6 h to ensure efficient purification. The final product, retained within the dialysis bag, was freez-dried and stored at 4 °C until further use. For comparison, NCDs were synthesized under the same conditions, except FeCl3•6H2O was omitted from the procedures. Metal-doped NCDs were synthesized using the corresponding metal chloride as the starting material.

The detection process involved mixing 250 µL of NCDs-Fe (0.38 mg/mL) with 250 µL of carbonate buffer (pH 10.0). To this mixture, 200 µL of 10.0 mM 4-ANP and 150 µL of 0.40 mM H2O2 were added. The solution was gently mixed for one minute, followed by the addition of varying amounts of CFD (150 µL). The reaction was carried out at 25 °C ± 1°C for three minutes. Absorption spectra were measured at 510 nm, while fluorescence readings were recorded at 460 nm after excitation at 370 nm.

The following steps were performed to study steady-state kinetics: 300 µL of NCDs-Fe (0.38 mg/mL) was combined with 500 µL of acetate buffer (pH 3.6) and 200 µL of varying concentrations of TMB and H2O2. The reaction was then conducted at 40 °C, with absorbance measurements taken every 30 s. The values of Vmax ​ and Km were determined using the Michaelis-Menten equation:
1 V
=
K
m
V
m
[
S
]
+
1
V
m
a
x
Where Km is the Michaelis constant, V is the initial rate of the reaction, Vmax​ is the maximum reaction velocity, and [S] is the substrate concentration.

Twenty Duricef® (500 mg CFD) tablets were finely powdered. A tablet-equivalent portion was dispersed in 50 mL deionized water, sonicated for 15 min, and diluted to 100 mL. The solution was filtered (0.22 µm), and the filtrate was further diluted to a concentration within the method’s dynamic linear range.

Heparinized blood samples from healthy volunteers were centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 20 min. After protein precipitation with 0.8 mL trichloroacetic acid, the supernatant was collected for analysis. CFD levels were determined pre- and post-addition of known concentrations. Sample handling followed Assiut University ethical guidelines.

The surface morphology of NCDs-Fe was examined using TEM, as shown in Fig. 1
A. The analysis revealed that the particles exhibited a spherical shape with a well-dispersed distribution. The inset of TEM image displays the HRTEM with lattice spacing of 0.23 A°, suggesting the graphitic-like pattern. The average particle size was determined using ImageJ software, yielding a mean diameter of 1.83 nm (n = 60), as illustrated in Fig. 1 B. The XRD data presented in Fig. 1 C reveals diffraction peaks at approximately 25.4° and 36.8°, corresponding to graphitic carbon and the Fe-N phase of NCDs-Fe, respectively [21,39]. Fig. 1 D presents the EDX pattern of the synthesized NCDs-Fe, displaying prominent peaks corresponding to C, N, O, and Fe, confirming the successful formation of NCDs-Fe. To highlight the key differences between NCDs and NCDs-Fe and confirm Fe doping during synthesis, FT-IR spectroscopy was performed, as shown in Fig. 2
A. Both NCDs and NCDs-Fe exhibited characteristic absorption bands at 3370, 2880, 1690 cm−1, corresponding to stretching vibrations of hydroxyl/amino, methylene, and carbonyl groups, respectively while and bending vibration of hydroxyl/amino groups appears at and 1603 cm−1. Additionally, a new absorption peak emerged at 680 cm−1 in NCDs-Fe, attributed to δ(O-Fe-N), confirming Fe incorporation [40]. These findings validate the successful Fe doping and the presence of polar functional groups responsible for water solubility. Additionally, Raman spectra were recorded for NCDs and NCDs-Fe, as shown in Fig. 2 B. Two characteristic bands were seen at 1392 cm−1 and 1594 cm−1, corresponding to the D-band (associated with structural disorder in sp2 carbon frameworks) and the G-band (linked to the stretching of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms in graphene and graphite), respectively [41,42]. The ID/IG ratios for NCDs and NCDs-Fe were calculated as 0.883 and 0.998, respectively, indicating a higher degree of disorder in the sp2 carbon structures of NCDs-Fe.

XPS analysis of NCDs-Fe was performed to investigate surface functional groups, binding states, and Fe valence states ( Fig. S1 ). The full-spectrum reveals peaks for C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, and Fe 2p, confirming elemental composition ( Fig. S1
A). Deconvoluted C 1 s spectra show bands at 282.7, 283.2, and 286.5 eV, corresponding to C–C, C=C, and C–O/C=N, respectively ( Fig. S1
B). The N 1 s spectrum displays peaks at 395.9, 397.5, 402.1, and 408.7 eV, assigned to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, N–Fe, and N-oxide, respectively, confirming nitrogen incorporation and interaction with Fe ( Fig. S1
C). Fig. S1
D presents the high-resolution O 1 s spectrum, revealing distinct peaks at 528.9 eV, 530.7 eV, 532.2 eV, and 533.2 eV, corresponding to C–O, Fe–O, C=O, and C–OH, respectively. These peaks confirm the presence of functional groups containing oxgygen and the Fe–O interaction in NCDs-Fe. Lastly, the high-resolution Fe 2p spectrum ( Fig. S1
E) exhibits prominent peaks at 712.3 eV, 719.7 eV, 724.7 eV, and 728.9 eV, corresponding to Fe2+ 2p3/2, a satellite peak, Fe2+ 2p1/2, and Fe3+ 2p1/2, respectively. These findings affirm the existence of both Fe2+ and Fe3+ oxidation states in NCDs-Fe [21,43].

The chemical structure and composition of the synthesized NCDs-Fe were analyzed using UV/Vis spectroscopy based on their characteristic absorption profiles. Fluorescence spectroscopy was used to analyze the excitation/emission wavelengths and fluorescence intensity (Fig. 3
A). The fluorescence spectra of NCDs-Fe were recorded in the 300–600 nm range following excitation at 370 nm, with the highest emission observed at 460 nm, which was used for subsequent measurements. Additionally, NCDs-Fe exhibited two absorption maxima at 230 nm and 310 nm, corresponding to π-π* and n-π* transitions, respectively, indicative of C=C and C=O/C=N functional groups in the structure. Fig. 3 B illustrates a redshift in NCDs-Fe emission with increasing excitation wavelength (330–400 nm), suggesting heterogeneity in functional groups and particle size [44]. The highest emission was obtained after excitation at 370 nm, which was used for subsequent determination of CFD. Additionally, the quantum yield of the as-synthesized NCDs-Fe was calculated to be 28.76 % against quinine sulfate at 360 nm (see
Supporting Information
for details). As shown in Fig. S2 , NCDs-Fe exhibited excellent stability under diverse conditions, including pH, Salt concentration, temperature, light exposure, and storage time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase-like activity of NCDs and NCDs-Fe
ce:section.ce:para:
The peroxidase-like activity of both NCDs and NCDs-Fe was evaluated using 4-ANP, H2O2, and the CFD template. In an alkaline medium, CFD and 4-ANP underwent oxidative coupling, forming a red-colored quinoneimine product with a maximum absorption at 510 nm. To confirm the role of NCDs-Fe as a catalytic site for this oxidative coupling, a series of colorimetric measurements was recorded ( Fig. S3 ). To validate these findings, UV/Vis absorption spectra were recorded for NCDs-Fe, NCDs-Fe/H2O2, NCDs-Fe/CFD, NCDs-Fe/4-ANP, and NCDs-Fe/H2O2/4-ANP/CFD ( Fig. S3
A). Notably, only NCDs-Fe/H2O2/4-ANP/CFD exhibited a strong absorption band at 510 nm, highlighting the critical role of NCDs-Fe in catalytic activity. These results align well with the data presented in Fig. S3
B. Furthermore, Fig. S3
C underscores the necessity of both H2O2 and NCDs-Fe for the oxidative coupling of 4-ANP and CFD. Additionally, Fig. S3
D demonstrates that substituting NCDs for NCDs-Fe did not yield an absorption band at 510 nm, confirming the essential contribution of Fe atoms to the catalytic functionality of the CDs. Additionally, various metal-doped nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were synthesized and tested for peroxidase-like activity (Fig. 4 ). Among them, Fe-doped NCDs demonstrated the highest catalytic efficiency. This superior activity is attributed to the strong electron transfer capabilities of Fe ions, which enhance the redox cycling between Fe2+/Fe3+ states, thereby facilitating the decomposition of H2O2. The result confirms the pivotal role of iron in mimicking peroxidase behavior and supports its selection for further sensor development.

The steady-state kinetic parameters of the as-prepared catalyst were demonstrated, as shown in Fig. S4 . The results revealed that the relationship between TMB and H2O2 followed a hyperbolic trend, consistent with the Michaelis-Menten model. The Vmax and Km values of the catalyst were calculated from the Lineweaver-Burk plot. As shown in Table S1 , the Vmax values for TMB and H2O2 were determined to be 11.29 × 10−8 M s−1 and 28.56 × 10−8 M s−1, respectively. Additionally, the Km ​values for TMB and H2O2 were found to be 0.156 mM and 0.016 mM, respectively. These results confirm the excellent catalytic affinity and activity of the as-prepared NCDs-Fe.

To enhance detection sensitivity, the parameters influencing the synthesis of NCDs-Fe and the detection of CFD were meticulously optimized ( Fig. S5 ). Figs. S5
A and S5B illustrate the effect of synthesis temperature, ranging from 140 °C to 260 °C, and synthesis duration, spanning 4 to 16 h, respectively. The highest fluorescence readings were seen at an optimal synthesis temperature of 220 °C and duration of 10 h. Consequently, these optimized conditions were utilized for the subsequent quantitative estimation of CFD. Furthermore, key parameters influencing the detection of CFD were systematically optimized, including the amounts of 4-ANP/H2O2, reaction time, and pH value. As depicted in Fig. S5
C, the effect of 4-ANP concentration was evaluated within the range of 5.0–20.0 mM. The maximum quenching effect for NCDs-Fe was observed at an optimal concentration of 10.0 mM. Below this threshold, the 4-ANP concentration was insufficient to effectively drive the chemical reaction. Conversely, at concentrations exceeding 10.0 mM, unreacted 4-ANP underwent oxidation by H2O2, generating blank readings that interfered with the fluorescence of NCDs-Fe. Similarly, Fig. S5
D illustrates the influence of H2O2 concentration within the range of 0.1–0.8 mM. Maximum response occurred at 0.4 mM. Below this level, the amount of H2O2 was inadequate to sustain the chemical reaction, while concentrations exceeding this threshold led to the disappearance of the characteristic pink coloration, thereby affecting the assay's accuracy. Fig. S5
E showcases the effect of reaction time within the range of 1–8 min. The optimal response was observed at 3.0 min, beyond which the responses stabilized. Thus, this duration was chosen for subsequent analyses to ensure accuracy and reproducibility. Finally, the influence of the pH value of the carbonate buffer was evaluated within the range of 8.0–12.0 ( Fig. S5
F). Maximum response was achieved at pH 10. Below this value, the pH conditions may not be suitable for effective chemical interaction between CFD and 4-ANP. Conversely, at pH levels exceeding 10, the response decreased, likely due to the instability of colorimetric reactions at higher pH values, which can affect the formation and stability of the colored products [37].

Dual-mode detection utilizing NCDs-Fe as a peroxidase-mimetic was employed to quantify CFD in a standard solution. Under optimized conditions, an increase in CFD concentration resulted in a proportional increase in absorbance at 510 nm, within the range of 0.05–400 µM (Fig. 5
A). For the fluorometric method, a higher CFD concentration led to enhanced quenching of NCDs fluorescence, with a detection range of 0.01–400 µM (Fig. 5 B). The detection limits, determined at S/N = 3, were calculated as 0.02 µM for the colorimetric method and 0.004 µM for the fluorometric method. Regression equations were derived by plotting absorbance vs. concentration and F0/F vs. concentration for the respective methods. The equations are as follows:
Colorimetric method: A = 0.0018[CFD] + 0.1433A (r2 = 0.9974) (Fig. 5 C)
Fluorometric method: [F0/F] = 0.008[CFD] + 1.0305[F0/F] (r2 = 0.9972) (Fig. 5 D)

Additionally, the proposed methods were evaluated against previously reported CFD determination techniques (Table 1 ). The comparison revealed that the developed methods offer wide linear range and a remarkably low detection limit, making them highly suitable for the accurate quantification of CFD in real samples. These advantages highlight the potential applicability of the proposed methods in practical analytical settings, ensuring reliability and sensitivity in CFD detection.

The reproducibility and stability of the peroxidase-like NCDs-Fe platform were assessed ( Fig. S6 ). For reproducibility, seven batches were synthesized under identical conditions and used to detect 20 µM CFD. The RSD was below 3.26 %, confirming good reproducibility ( Fig. S6
A). Stability was evaluated by monitoring relative catalytic activity over time ( Fig. S6
B), with results showing maintained activity for up to 65 days, indicating strong long-term stability.

The selectivity of the analytical method is a crucial parameter to demonstrate its performance in detecting the target analyte in real samples. Therefore, the NCDs-Fe catalyst was evaluated for its potential selectivity and anti-interference ability by detecting 20 µM CFD in the presence of a 25-fold excess of interfering species. These interfering species include various ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, SO4 2−, CO3 2−), small biomolecules (dopamine), and other compounds such as ascorbic acid, uric acid, glutathione, and cysteine, as well as antibiotics (penicillin, ampicillin, cefazolin, cephalexin, cefuroxime, cefaclor, cefoxitin, ceftriaxone, cefotaxime, ceftazidime, cefdinir, cefepime, cefixime, ceftobiprole, and ceftaroline fosamil). It was observed that only CFD produced significant quenching of the NCDs-Fe signal, indicating the high selectivity and strong anti-interference capability of the catalyst (Fig. 6 ).

The fluorescence quenching of NCDs-Fe was significantly enhanced in the presence of H2O2, 4-ANP, and CFD ( Fig. S7
A). To elucidate the quenching mechanism between the formed colored product (quinoneimine) and NCDs-Fe, absorption and fluorescence spectra were recorded. The absorption spectrum of the resulting quinoneimine product, peaking at 510 nm, overlaps with the emission spectrum of NCDs-Fe (460 nm), indicating the presence of an inner-filter effect (IFE) between them ( Fig. S7
B). Additionally, fluorescence lifetime measurements of NCDs-Fe before and after the addition of H2O2/4-ANP/CFD were recorded as 5.74 ns and 5.76 ns, respectively, suggesting the occurrence of static quenching ( Fig. S7
C) [48–51]. In conclusion, the quenching of NCDs-Fe results from a combination of IFE and static quenching.

To validate the dual-mode NCDs-Fe platform, the standard addition method was applied to pharmaceutical tablets and CFD-spiked serum samples. Measured concentrations closely matched the spiked values, with recovery rates ranging from 97.5 % to 104.0 % and RSDs below 4.08 %, indicating high accuracy and reliability (Tables 2 & 3
). These results confirm the practical applicability of the method for real-world CFD detection.

A dual-mode approach for detecting cefadroxil (CFD) was developed using the peroxidase-mimetic activity of NCDs-Fe. Unlike NCDs alone, NCDs-Fe significantly enhanced the oxidation of 4-aminoantipyrine (4-ANP), facilitating its oxidative coupling with phenolic CFD to form a red-colored product with an absorption peak at 510 nm. The colorimetric approach relies on measuring the absorbance of this red-colored product at 510 nm, which increases proportionally with the CFD concentration. In the fluorometric approach, the red-colored product quenches the fluorescence of NCDs-Fe at 460 nm. The detection strategies exhibit a wide linear range, low detection limits, good selectivity, and high reliability. Both techniques were successfully applied to determine the concentration of CFD in real samples using the standard addition method. This study presents a simple method for fabricating NCDs-Fe with strong peroxidase-mimicking activity and intense fluorescence, making them effective nanozymes for CFD detection via both colorimetric and fluorometric approaches.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2025.126313.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antibiotic resistance in the environment
Nat. Rev. Microbiol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.G.J. Larsson, C.-F. Flach, Antibiotic resistance in the environment. Nat. Rev. Microbiol., 20 (5) (2022), pp. 257-269.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antibiotic resistance in microbes: History, mechanisms, therapeutic strategies and future prospects
J. Infect. Public Health

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. M. Uddin, A. J. Chakraborty, A. Khusro, R. M. Zidan, S. Mitra, T. B. Emran, K. Dhama, K.H. Ripon, M. Gajdács,K. Sahibzada, J. Hossain, N. Koirala, Antibiotic resistance in microbes: History, mechanisms, therapeutic strategies and future prospects. Journal of Infection and Public Health, 14 (12) (2021), pp. 1750-1766.
